source_paper_title: Can Learning Be Explained by Local Optimality in Robust Low-Rank Matrix Recovery?
context:
Page 12, Section 2.3 (Asymmetric matrix completion), discussion after Theorem 6

“We note that this result does not refute the possibility of existing highly rank-imbalanced solutions—in particular, those with \(\operatorname{rank}(W_1^\star)+\operatorname{rank}(W_2^\star)\ge k\)—as local optima. Although our result does not refute this possibility, we conjecture that highly rank-imbalanced solutions are unlikely to emerge as local optima. We leave a rigorous proof of this conjecture for future work.”
title: Prove that highly rank-imbalanced true solutions are not local optima in asymmetric matrix completion with ℓ1-loss
latex:
Given integers \(d_1,d_2\ge 1\), true rank \(r\le \min\{d_1,d_2\}\), and search rank \(k>r\), let the ground-truth matrix be \(X^\star\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) with \(\mathrm{rank}(X^\star)=r\). Let \(\Omega\subseteq[d_1]\times[d_2]\) be a (possibly full) set of observed indices and define the (possibly noisy) observation matrix \(Y\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) by
\[
Y[\alpha,\beta]=X^\star[\alpha,\beta]+E[\alpha,\beta]\quad\text{for }(\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega,
\]
where \(E\) is a noise matrix supported on \(\Omega\). Consider the Burer--Monteiro factorization variables \(W_1\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times k}\), \(W_2\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times d_2}\) and the nonsmooth \(\ell_1\) matrix-completion loss
\[
f_{\ell_1}^c(W_1,W_2):=\frac{1}{|\Omega|}\sum_{(\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega} \big|Y[\alpha,\beta]-(W_1W_2)[\alpha,\beta]\big|.
\]
Define the set of true solutions
\[
\mathcal{W}:=\{(W_1,W_2): W_1W_2=X^\star\}.
\]
Call a true solution \((W_1^\star,W_2^\star)\in\mathcal{W}\) highly rank-imbalanced if \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1^\star)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2^\star)\ge k\).

Problem: Under the matrix completion model for \(\Omega\) and a generic noise model with a constant fraction of corrupted observations, prove (or disprove) that, with high probability, no highly rank-imbalanced true solution \((W_1^\star,W_2^\star)\in\mathcal{W}\) is a local minimizer of \(f_{\ell_1}^c\). Equivalently, establish conditions under which for every highly rank-imbalanced \((W_1^\star,W_2^\star)\in\mathcal{W}\) there exists \(\Delta W=(\Delta W_1,\Delta W_2)\) with \(\|\Delta W\|_F=1\) and some \(\bar\gamma>0\) such that
\[
f_{\ell_1}^c(W_1^\star+\gamma\Delta W_1,\, W_2^\star+\gamma\Delta W_2) < f_{\ell_1}^c(W_1^\star,W_2^\star)\quad\text{for all }\gamma\in(0,\bar\gamma].
\]
location_in_paper: Page 12, Section 2.3 (Asymmetric matrix completion), discussion after Theorem 6
open_problem_quote: “We note that this result does not refute the possibility of existing highly rank-imbalanced solutions—in particular, those with \(\operatorname{rank}(W_1^\star)+\operatorname{rank}(W_2^\star)\ge k\)—as local optima. Although our result does not refute this possibility, we conjecture that highly rank-imbalanced solutions are unlikely to emerge as local optima. We leave a rigorous proof of this conjecture for future work.”
subject_classification: Nonconvex Optimization / Robust Low-Rank Matrix Recovery
